dataset_info.title: History of Lok Sabha Elections — 1st to 18th (1951–2024)
dataset_info.description: Complete history of all 18 Indian General Elections (Lok Sabha), including winning parties, seat counts, vote percentages, and key political trends

lok_sabha_overview.type: Lower House of the Parliament of India
lok_sabha_overview.voting_eligibility: All adult Indian citizens aged 18 and above
lok_sabha_overview.meeting_location: Lok Sabha Chambers, Sansad Bhavan, New Delhi
lok_sabha_overview.purpose: Creation, removal, and improvement of laws affecting all citizens of India
lok_sabha_overview.dissolution_authority: President of India, on advice of the Council of Ministers
lok_sabha_overview.text: The Lok Sabha is India's lower house of Parliament with 543 elected seats. Elections are held every 5 years and all adult citizens aged 18+ can vote. It meets at Sansad Bhavan, New Delhi to legislate national laws. The first general election was held in 1951-52 and as of 2024, 18 Lok Sabhas have been elected.

lok_sabha_elections.key_fact: India's first ever general election after independence. Universal adult suffrage adopted. 105 million votes cast.
lok_sabha_elections.text: 1st Lok Sabha (1951-52): India's first general election. Indian National Congress won 364 out of 489 seats with 44.99% vote share. PM: Jawaharlal Nehru. CPI was 2nd with 16 seats. Over 105 million votes cast in the world's first large-scale democratic election with universal adult suffrage.

lok_sabha_elections.key_fact: Congress strengthened its majority. CPI doubled its seats from 16 to 27.
lok_sabha_elections.text: 2nd Lok Sabha (1957): Indian National Congress increased majority to 371 seats (47.78%) under PM Jawaharlal Nehru. CPI grew from 16 to 27 seats (8.92%). Praja Socialist Party ranked 3rd with 19 seats.

lok_sabha_elections.key_fact: Indelible ink practice introduced in this election. Swatantra Party emerged as new right-wing force. Held just before the 1962 India-China war.
lok_sabha_elections.text: 3rd Lok Sabha (1962): Congress won 361 seats (44.72%) under PM Nehru. CPI grew to 29 seats (9.94%). Swatantra Party emerged as 3rd force with 18 seats (7.89%). Indelible ink was introduced in this election.

lok_sabha_elections.key_fact: Congress majority significantly reduced. Indira Gandhi became PM for the first time. Rise of opposition parties signaled end of Congress dominance.
lok_sabha_elections.text: 4th Lok Sabha (1967): Congress majority dropped sharply to 283 seats (40.78%). First election under PM Indira Gandhi. Swatantra Party 2nd with 44 seats. Bharatiya Jana Sangh (BJP's predecessor) 3rd with 35 seats — early signs of Congress decline.

lok_sabha_elections.key_fact: Famous 'Garibi Hatao' (Remove Poverty) campaign by Indira Gandhi. Congress split into INC(R) — Indira's faction — and INC(O). Indira's faction won a landslide.
lok_sabha_elections.text: 5th Lok Sabha (1971): INC(R) — Indira Gandhi's faction — won 352 seats (43.68%) on the 'Garibi Hatao' (Remove Poverty) slogan. CPI(M) 2nd with 25 seats. Election followed the Congress party split of 1969.

lok_sabha_elections.key_fact: Historic election — first time Congress lost power after independence. Held after the Emergency period (1975-77). Anti-incumbency wave swept out Indira Gandhi. PM Morarji Desai became India's first non-Congress PM.
lok_sabha_elections.text: 6th Lok Sabha (1977): Janata Party won 295 seats (41.32%), ending 30 years of Congress rule. First non-Congress government — PM Morarji Desai. Held after the controversial Emergency period (1975-77) declared by Indira Gandhi. Congress fell to 154 seats.

lok_sabha_elections.key_fact: Indira Gandhi made a triumphant comeback after the Janata government collapsed due to internal splits. Congress(I) swept to power with 353 seats.
lok_sabha_elections.text: 7th Lok Sabha (1980): Congress(I) returned to power with 353 seats (42.69%) under Indira Gandhi after the Janata government collapsed. Janata Party (Secular) distant 2nd with only 41 seats. CPI(M) 3rd with 37 seats.

lok_sabha_elections.key_fact: Largest Lok Sabha majority ever. Held after assassination of PM Indira Gandhi. Sympathy wave for Congress. Rajiv Gandhi became PM at age 40. TDP emerged as major regional party.
lok_sabha_elections.text: 8th Lok Sabha (1984): Congress(I) won its biggest-ever majority — 414 seats (48.12%) — riding a sympathy wave after PM Indira Gandhi's assassination. Rajiv Gandhi became PM. TDP emerged as a new major regional party with 30 seats.

lok_sabha_elections.key_fact: Congress lost majority drastically. BJP emerged with 85 seats — first major breakthrough. V.P. Singh formed minority government with outside support. Rise of Mandal Commission politics.
lok_sabha_elections.text: 9th Lok Sabha (1989): Congress collapsed from 414 to 197 seats. V.P. Singh's Janata Dal (143 seats) formed a minority government with BJP's outside support. BJP's rise to 85 seats (11.36%) marked its emergence as a major national force.

lok_sabha_elections.key_fact: Held after assassination of Rajiv Gandhi. PM P.V. Narasimha Rao launched landmark economic liberalisation. BJP surged to 120 seats, cementing its status as main opposition. First minority Congress government.
lok_sabha_elections.text: 10th Lok Sabha (1991): Congress won 244 seats (36.40%) — a minority government under PM P.V. Narasimha Rao, who initiated India's economic liberalisation. BJP surged to 120 seats (20.07%), solidifying its national presence. Election was held mid-way after Rajiv Gandhi's assassination.

lok_sabha_elections.prime_minister: H. D. Deve Gowda (then I. K. Gujral)
lok_sabha_elections.key_fact: First time BJP became the single largest party. No single party had majority. BJP's government lasted only 13 days. United Front coalition government formed with Congress support from outside.
lok_sabha_elections.text: 11th Lok Sabha (1996): BJP first became the single largest party with 161 seats (20.29%), surpassing Congress (140 seats). No party won majority. BJP's government lasted only 13 days. A United Front coalition led by H.D. Deve Gowda formed the government with Congress support.

lok_sabha_elections.key_fact: BJP formed coalition NDA government under Vajpayee. Government fell after 13 months when AIADMK withdrew support. India conducted Pokhran-II nuclear tests under this government.
lok_sabha_elections.text: 12th Lok Sabha (1998): BJP won 182 seats (25.59%), forming an NDA coalition government under PM Atal Bihari Vajpayee. Congress narrowly close with 141 seats (25.82%). Government lasted 13 months before collapse. India conducted Pokhran-II nuclear tests during this term.

lok_sabha_elections.key_fact: First NDA government to complete a full 5-year term. Notable for Kargil War (1999), highway and telecom expansion, and economic growth. India Shining campaign launched ahead of 2004.
lok_sabha_elections.text: 13th Lok Sabha (1999): BJP retained 182 seats (23.75%) forming a stable NDA coalition under PM Vajpayee — the first non-Congress government to complete a full 5-year term. Marked by Kargil War, economic growth, and the 'India Shining' narrative.

lok_sabha_elections.key_fact: Surprise upset — BJP's 'India Shining' campaign backfired. Congress returned to power as part of UPA coalition with Left support. Dr. Manmohan Singh became PM, serving for 10 years (2004–2014). Right to Information Act and NREGA launched.
lok_sabha_elections.text: 14th Lok Sabha (2004): Congress upset the NDA with 145 seats (26.53%) vs BJP's 138. UPA coalition formed under PM Dr. Manmohan Singh. BJP's 'India Shining' campaign failed to resonate. Congress-led UPA passed landmark legislation including RTI Act and NREGA.

lok_sabha_elections.key_fact: Congress significantly strengthened, no longer needing Left support. BJP hit a historic low of 116 seats. UPA-II governed amid 2G spectrum scandal and anti-corruption movements led by Anna Hazare.
lok_sabha_elections.text: 15th Lok Sabha (2009): Congress improved to 206 seats (28.55%) under PM Manmohan Singh's second term. BJP fell to a historic low of 116 seats (18.80%). UPA-II governed without Left support but faced major corruption controversies.

lok_sabha_elections.third_party: All India Anna Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam
lok_sabha_elections.key_fact: BJP won first single-party majority since Rajiv Gandhi's 1984 landslide. Congress collapsed to its worst-ever result — 44 seats. VVPAT introduced in 8 pilot constituencies. Narendra Modi became PM. Strong anti-incumbency and Modi wave.
lok_sabha_elections.text: 16th Lok Sabha (2014): BJP won a historic landslide — 282 seats (31.34%) — first single-party majority in 30 years. Congress fell to its worst-ever 44 seats (19.52%). PM Narendra Modi took office for his first term. VVPAT piloted in 8 constituencies in this election.

lok_sabha_elections.key_fact: BJP strengthened majority to 303 seats, largest NDA mandate ever. VVPAT made mandatory in all polling stations. Congress slightly improved to 52 seats but far from major opposition. Pulwama attack and Balakot airstrikes shaped election narrative.
lok_sabha_elections.text: 17th Lok Sabha (2019): BJP won its biggest-ever mandate — 303 seats (37.70%) under PM Modi's second term. Congress improved slightly to 52 seats. VVPAT became mandatory nationwide. Supreme Court ordered verification of 5 random EVMs per assembly constituency.

lok_sabha_elections.key_fact: BJP lost outright majority (272 needed), winning 240 seats — forced into coalition dependence on TDP and JDU. Congress made major comeback from 52 to 99 seats. INDIA Alliance emerged as strong opposition. Total votes cast: 614 million — largest election in world history. ECI extended vote-from-home to those aged 85+ and PwD for the first time.
lok_sabha_elections.text: 18th Lok Sabha (2024): BJP won 240 seats (36.56%), falling short of 272 majority — first time since 2014 BJP needed coalition partners (TDP, JDU). PM Modi began his third term. Congress rebounded to 99 seats. Samajwadi Party 3rd with 37 seats. 614 million votes cast — largest democratic election ever. NOTA recorded 6.37 million votes (1.04%).

lok_sabha_trends.description: Indian National Congress dominated all five elections, consistently winning more than 280 seats and over 40% vote share. Jawaharlal Nehru won the first three; Indira Gandhi the 4th and 5th.
lok_sabha_trends.text: From 1952 to 1971 (1st to 5th Lok Sabha), Indian National Congress dominated Indian politics, winning 283–371 seats per election with 40–48% vote share. PM Nehru led the first three victories; PM Indira Gandhi the next two.

lok_sabha_trends.description: Post-Emergency elections saw a massive anti-incumbency wave. Janata Party formed India's first non-Congress government under Morarji Desai. Congress reduced to 154 seats.
lok_sabha_trends.text: The 6th Lok Sabha (1977) was a watershed moment — the first time Congress lost power since independence. Held after the controversial Emergency (1975-77), the Janata Party won 295 seats and PM Morarji Desai formed India's first non-Congress government.

lok_sabha_trends.description: Congress returned with Indira Gandhi in 1980. After her assassination in 1984, Congress won its biggest-ever majority of 414 seats under Rajiv Gandhi on a sympathy wave.
lok_sabha_trends.text: The 7th and 8th Lok Sabhas saw Congress dominance restored. Indira Gandhi returned in 1980 (353 seats). After her assassination, the 1984 election gave Congress its all-time record of 414 seats (48.12%) under Rajiv Gandhi — the largest majority in Indian electoral history.

lok_sabha_trends.topic: Rise of BJP and Coalition Era (1989–2004)
lok_sabha_trends.description: BJP grew from 2 seats in 1984 to 182 seats by 1998. Coalition governments became the norm. No party won outright majority from 1989 to 2014. BJP formed the first coalition government to complete a full 5-year term (13th Lok Sabha, 1999–2004).
lok_sabha_trends.text: The 9th to 14th Lok Sabhas (1989–2004) ushered in the era of coalition politics. BJP grew steadily from 85 seats (1989) to 182 seats (1998-99). The NDA under Vajpayee completed India's first full non-Congress 5-year term. No single party won an outright majority in this entire period.

lok_sabha_trends.description: BJP won its first single-party majority in 30 years in 2014 under Narendra Modi (282 seats). Strengthened to 303 seats in 2019. In 2024, BJP fell below majority threshold to 240 seats but retained power through coalition. Congress rebounded from 44 to 99 seats by 2024.
lok_sabha_trends.text: From 2014 to 2024 (16th–18th Lok Sabhas), BJP dominated under PM Narendra Modi. BJP won single-party majorities in 2014 (282 seats) and 2019 (303 seats) — the first since Rajiv Gandhi's 1984 victory. In 2024, BJP dropped to 240 seats, needing TDP and JDU coalition support. Congress recovered from 44 (2014) to 52 (2019) to 99 seats (2024).
